audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man phoning to find out about some children's engineering workshops.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, children's engineering workshops.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: I wanted some information about the workshops in the school holidays.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: I have two daughters who are interested.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: The younger one's Lydia.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: She's 4.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Do you take children as young as that?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Yes, our Tiny Engineers workshop is for 4 to 5 year olds.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: What sorts of activities do they do?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: All sorts.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: For example, they work together to design a special cover that goes round an egg, so that when it's inside, they can drop it from a height, and it doesn't break.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Well, sometimes it does break, but that's part of the fun.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: And Lydia loves building things.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Is there any opportunity for her to do that?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Well, they have a competition to see who can make the highest tower.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: You'd be amazed how high they can go.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: But they're learning all the time as well as having fun.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: For example, one thing they do is to design and build a car that's attached to a balloon.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And the force of the air in that actually powers the car, and makes it move along.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: They go really fast too.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: OK, well, all this sounds perfect.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now Carly, that's my older daughter, has just had her 7th birthday.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: So presumably she'd be in a different group.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: She'd be in the Junior Engineers.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: That's for children from 6 to 8.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And do they do the same sorts of activities?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Some are the same.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: But a bit more advanced.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: So they work out how to build model vehicles, things like cars and trucks, but also how to construct animals using the same sorts of material and technique.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And then they learn how they can program them and make them move.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: So they learn a bit of coding.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: They do.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: They pick it up really quickly.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: We're there to help if they need it, but they learn from one another too.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: And do they have competitions too?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Yes, with the Junior Engineers, it's to use recycled materials like card and wood.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: To build a bridge, and the longest one gets a prize.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: That sounds fun, I wouldn't mind doing that myself.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Then they have something a bit different.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Which is to think up an idea for a 5 minute movie, and then film it using special animation software.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: You'd be amazed what they come up with.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And of course that's something they can put on their phone,
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: and take home to show all their friends.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And then they also build a robot in the shape of a human, and they decorate it and program it so that it can move its arms and legs.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Perfect.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: So is it the same price as the tiny engineers?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: It's just a bit more, £50 for the 5 weeks.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And are the classes on a Monday too?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: They used to be, but we found it didn't give our staff enough time to clear up after the first workshop, so we moved them to Wednesdays.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: The classes are held in the morning from 10 to 11.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: OK, that's better for me actually.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: And what about the location?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Where exactly are the workshops held?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: They're in building 10A.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: There's a big sign on the door, you can't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And that's in Fradstone Industrial Estate.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Sorry?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Fradstone.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: That's FRADSTONE.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And that's in Grasford, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: Yes, up past the station.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And will I have any parking problems there?
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: No, there's always plenty available.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: So would you like to enroll Lydia and Carly now?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: FATHER

audioscript.text: So can I have your full name?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Stevenson's Metal Goods Company and Work Experience Program

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear the Managing Director of a company called Stevenson's welcoming a group of work experience students.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone, and welcome to Stevenson's, one of the country's major manufacturers of metal goods.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Thank you for choosing us for your two weeks of work experience.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: My name is Julia Simmons, and since the beginning of this year, I've been the managing director.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Stevenson's is quite an old company.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Like me, the founder Ronald Stevenson went into the steel industry when he left school.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: That was in 1923.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: He set up this company when he finished his apprenticeship, in 1926, although he actually started making plans two years earlier, in 1924. He was a very determined young man!
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Stevenson's long-term plan was to manufacture components for the machine tools industry, although in fact that never came about, and for the automotive industry, that is, cars and lorries.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: However, there was a delay of five years before that happened.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Because shortly before the company went into production, Stevenson was given the opportunity to make goods for hospitals.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: And other players in the health care industry, so that's what we did for the first five years.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Over the years, we've expanded the premises considerably.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: We were lucky that the site is big enough, so moving to a new location has never been necessary.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: However, the layout is far from ideal for modern machinery and production methods, so we intend to carry out major refurbishment of this site over the next five years.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: I'd better give you some idea of what you'll be doing during your two weeks with us, so you know what to expect.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Most mornings you'll have a presentation from one of the managers.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: To learn about their department, starting this morning with research and development.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: And you'll all spend some time in each department, observing what's going on and talking to people,
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: as long as you don't stop them from doing their work altogether! In the past, a teacher from your school has come in at the end of each week to find out how the group were getting on.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: But your school isn't able to arrange that this year.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, now I'll briefly help you to orientate yourselves around the site.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: As you can see, we're in the reception area, which we try to make attractive and welcoming to visitors.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: There's a corridor running left from here, and if you go along that, the door facing you at the end is the entrance to the coffee room.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: This looks out onto the main road on one side, and some trees on the other.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: And that'll be where you meet each morning.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: The factory is the very big room on the far side of the site.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Next to it is the warehouse, which can be accessed by lorries going up the road to the turning area at the end.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: You can get to the warehouse by crossing to the far side of the courtyard, and then the door is on your right.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Somewhere you'll be keen to find is the staff canteen.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: This is right next to reception.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: I can confidently say that the food's very good, but the view isn't.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: The windows on one side look onto a corridor and courtyard.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Which aren't very attractive at all, and on the other onto the access road, which isn't much better.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: You'll be using the meeting room quite often, and you'll find it by walking along the corridor to the left of the courtyard, and continuing along it to the end.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: The meeting room is the last one on the right, and I'm afraid there's no natural daylight in the room.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Then you'll need to know where some of the offices are.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: The human resources department is at the front of this building, so you head to the left along the corridor from reception.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: And it's the second room you come to, it looks out onto the main road.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: And finally the board room where you'll be meeting sometimes.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: That has quite a pleasant view, as it looks out onto the trees.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Go along the corridor past the courtyard, right to the end.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: The boardroom is on the left.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: Next to the factory.
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: OK, now are there any questions before we...
audioscript.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: The factory is the very big room on the far side of the site.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: Next to it is the warehouse, which can be accessed by lorries going up the road to the turning area at the end.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: You can get to the warehouse by crossing to the far side of the courtyard, and then the door is on your right.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: Somewhere you'll be keen to find is the staff canteen.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: This is right next to reception.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: I can confidently say that the food's very good, but the view isn't.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: The windows on one side look onto a corridor and courtyard.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: Which aren't very attractive at all, and on the other onto the access road, which isn't much better.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: You'll be using the meeting room quite often, and you'll find it by walking along the corridor to the left of the courtyard, and continuing along it to the end.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: The meeting room is the last one on the right, and I'm afraid there's no natural daylight in the room.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: Then you'll need to know where some of the offices are.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: The human resources department is at the front of this building, so you head to the left along the corridor from reception.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: And it's the second room you come to, it looks out onto the main road.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: And finally the board room where you'll be meeting sometimes.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: That has quite a pleasant view, as it looks out onto the trees.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: Go along the corridor past the courtyard, right to the end.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: The boardroom is on the left.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: Next to the factory.
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: OK, now are there any questions before we...
utterances.speaker: JULIA SIMMONS

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What does the speaker say about the company premises?
options: The company has no plans to move.
The company is going to move shortly.

items.title: The programme for the work experience group includes

items: coffee room     <input>
warehouse       <input>
staff canteen   <input>
meeting room    <input>
human resources <input>
boardroom       <input>
body.headline: Plan of Stevenson's site

test_text.title: Discussing an Art Project on Birds

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two students called Jess and Tom discussing their art projects.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: How are you getting on with your art project, Tom?
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: OK, like they gave us the theme of birds to base our project on, and I'm not really all that interested in wildlife.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: But I'm starting to get into it.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I've pretty well finished the introductory stage.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: So have I.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: When they gave us all those handouts with details of books and websites to look at, I was really put off.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: But the more I read, the more interested I got.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Hmm. Me too.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I found I could research so many different aspects of birds in art - color, movement, texture.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: So I was looking forward to the bird park visit.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: What a letdown.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: It poured with rain, and we hardly saw a single bird.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Much less use than the trip to the Natural History Museum.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I liked all the stuff about evolution there.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: The workshop sessions with Dr Fletcher were good too, especially the brainstorming sessions.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Oh. I missed those because I was ill.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: I wish we could have seen the projects last year's students did.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Hmm. I suppose they want us to do our own thing, not copy.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Have you drafted your proposal yet?
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Yes, but I haven't handed it in.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I need to amend some parts.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I've realized the notes from my research are almost all just descriptions.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I haven't actually evaluated anything, so I'll have to fix that.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Oh, I didn't know we had to do that.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: I'll have to look at that too.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Did you do a timeline for the project?
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Yes, and a mind map.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Yeah, so did I.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: I quite enjoyed that.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: But it was hard having to explain the basis for my decisions in my action plan.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: What?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: You know, give a rationale.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: I didn't realize we had to do that.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: OK, I can add it now, and I've done the video diary presentation, and worked out what I want my outcome to be in the project.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Someone told me it's best not to be too precise about your actual outcome at this stage, so you have more scope to explore your ideas later on.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: So I'm going to go back to my proposal to make it a bit more vague.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: OK, I'll change that too then.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: One part of the project I'm unsure about is where we choose some paintings of birds, and say what they mean to us.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Like, I chose a painting of a falcon by Landseer.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I like it because the bird's standing there with his head turned to one side, but he seems to be staring straight at you.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: But I can't just say it's a bit scary, can I?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Hmm. You could talk about the possible danger suggested by the bird's look.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Oh, OK.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: There's a picture of a fish hawk by Audubon I like.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: It's swooping over the water with a fish in its talons, and with great black wings which take up most of the picture.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: So you could discuss it in relation to predators and food chains.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Well, actually, I think I'll concentrate on the impression of rapid motion it gives.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Do you know that picture of a kingfisher by van Gosh?
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: It's perching on a reed growing near a stream.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Yes, it's got these beautiful blue and red and black shades.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Hmm. I've actually chosen it because I saw a real kingfisher once, when I was little.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: I was out walking with my grandfather, and I've never forgotten it.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Oh, so we can use a personal link?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: There's a portrait called William Wells.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I can't remember the artist, but it's a middle aged man who's just shot a bird, and his expression, and the way he's holding the bird in his hand, suggests he's not sure about what he's done.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: To me, it's about how ambiguous people are in the way they exploit the natural world.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Interesting.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: There's Gauguin's picture Vairumati.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: He did it in Tahiti.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: It's a woman with a White Bird behind her that is eating a lizard, and what I'm interested in is what idea this bird refers to.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Apparently, it's a reference to the never ending cycle of existence.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: Wow.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I chose a portrait of a little boy, Giovanni de Medici.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: He's holding a tiny bird in one fist.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I like the way he's holding it carefully, so he doesn't hurt it.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Uh. Right.
audioscript.speaker: JESS

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

options: the projects done in previous years

body.title: Pictures

test_text.title: The Significance and Influence of Stoicism Throughout History

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a professor of philosophy giving a talk on the ancient philosophy of Stoicism.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Ancient philosophy is not just about talking or lecturing, or even reading long dense books.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, it is something people have used throughout history.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To solve their problems and to achieve their greatest triumphs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Specifically, I am referring to Stoicism, which in my opinion is the most practical of all philosophies, and therefore the most appealing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stoicism was founded in ancient Greece by Zeno of Citium in the early 3rd century BC, but was practiced by the likes of Epictetus.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Cato, Seneca, and Marcus Aurelius.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Amazingly, we still have access to these ideas, despite the fact that the most famous Stoics never wrote anything down for publication.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Cato definitely didn't, Marcus Aurelius never intended his Meditations to be anything but personal, Seneca's letters were, well, letters.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And Epictetus' thoughts come to us by way of a note-taking student.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stoic principles were based on the idea that its followers could have an unshakable happiness in this life, and the key to achieving this was virtue.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The road to virtue in turn lay in understanding that destructive emotions like anger and jealousy are under our conscious control.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They don't have to control us, because we can learn to control them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the words of Epictetus, external events I cannot control, but the choices I make with regard to them, I do control.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The modern day philosopher and writer, Nassim Nicholas Taleb, defines a Stoic as someone who has a different perspective on experiences,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: which most of us would see as wholly negative.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A Stoic "transforms fear into caution, pain into transformation, mistakes into initiation, and desire into undertaking". Using this definition as a model, we can see that throughout the centuries Stoicism has been practiced in more recent history by kings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Presidents, artists, writers, and entrepreneurs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The founding fathers of the United States were inspired by the philosophy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: George Washington was introduced to Stoicism by his neighbors at age 17.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And later put on a play based on the life of Cato to inspire his men.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Thomas Jefferson kept a copy of Seneca beside his bed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Writers and artists have also been inspired by the Stoics, Eugène Delacroix, the renowned French Romantic artist, known best for his painting Liberty Leading the People, was an ardent Stoic, referring to it as his consoling religion.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The economist Adam Smith's theories on capitalism were significantly influenced by the Stoicism that he studied as a schoolboy, under a teacher who had translated Marcus Aurelius' works.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today's political leaders are no different, with many finding their inspiration from the ancient texts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Former US President Bill Clinton rereads Marcus Aurelius every single year, and many have compared former President Obama's calm leadership style to that of Cato.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Wen Jiabao, the former prime minister of China, claims that Meditations is one of two books he travels with, and that he has read it more than 100 times over the course of his life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stoicism had a profound influence on Albert Ellis, who invented cognitive behavior therapy, which is used to help people manage their problems,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: by changing the way that they think and behave.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's most commonly used to treat depression.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The idea is that we can take control of our lives by challenging the irrational beliefs
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: that create our faulty thinking symptoms and behaviors by using logic instead.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stoicism has also become popular in the world of business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Stoic principles can build the resilience and state of mind required to overcome setbacks, because Stoics teach turning obstacles into opportunity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A lesson every business entrepreneur needs to learn.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I would argue that studying Stoicism is as relevant today as it was 2,000 years ago, thanks to its brilliant insights into how to lead a good life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the very root of the thinking, there is a very simple way of living.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Control what you can, and accept what you can't.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is not as easy as it sounds, and will require considerable practice.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It can take a lifetime to master.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Stoics also believed the most important foundation for a good and happy life is not money, fame, power or pleasure, but having a disciplined and principled character.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Something which seems to resonate with many people today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To solve their problems and to achieve their greatest triumphs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Specifically, I am referring to Stoicism, which in my opinion is the most practical of all philosophies, and therefore the most appealing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Stoicism was founded in ancient Greece by Zeno of Citium in the early 3rd century BC, but was practiced by the likes of Epictetus.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Cato, Seneca, and Marcus Aurelius.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Amazingly, we still have access to these ideas, despite the fact that the most famous Stoics never wrote anything down for publication.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Cato definitely didn't, Marcus Aurelius never intended his Meditations to be anything but personal, Seneca's letters were, well, letters.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And Epictetus' thoughts come to us by way of a note-taking student.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Stoic principles were based on the idea that its followers could have an unshakable happiness in this life, and the key to achieving this was virtue.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The road to virtue in turn lay in understanding that destructive emotions like anger and jealousy are under our conscious control.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They don't have to control us, because we can learn to control them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the words of Epictetus, external events I cannot control, but the choices I make with regard to them, I do control.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The modern day philosopher and writer, Nassim Nicholas Taleb, defines a Stoic as someone who has a different perspective on experiences,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: which most of us would see as wholly negative.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A Stoic "transforms fear into caution, pain into transformation, mistakes into initiation, and desire into undertaking". Using this definition as a model, we can see that throughout the centuries Stoicism has been practiced in more recent history by kings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Presidents, artists, writers, and entrepreneurs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The founding fathers of the United States were inspired by the philosophy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: George Washington was introduced to Stoicism by his neighbors at age 17.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And later put on a play based on the life of Cato to inspire his men.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Thomas Jefferson kept a copy of Seneca beside his bed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Writers and artists have also been inspired by the Stoics, Eugène Delacroix, the renowned French Romantic artist, known best for his painting Liberty Leading the People, was an ardent Stoic, referring to it as his consoling religion.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The economist Adam Smith's theories on capitalism were significantly influenced by the Stoicism that he studied as a schoolboy, under a teacher who had translated Marcus Aurelius' works.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today's political leaders are no different, with many finding their inspiration from the ancient texts.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Former US President Bill Clinton rereads Marcus Aurelius every single year, and many have compared former President Obama's calm leadership style to that of Cato.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Wen Jiabao, the former prime minister of China, claims that Meditations is one of two books he travels with, and that he has read it more than 100 times over the course of his life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Stoicism had a profound influence on Albert Ellis, who invented cognitive behavior therapy, which is used to help people manage their problems,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: by changing the way that they think and behave.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's most commonly used to treat depression.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The idea is that we can take control of our lives by challenging the irrational beliefs
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: that create our faulty thinking symptoms and behaviors by using logic instead.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Stoicism has also become popular in the world of business.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Stoic principles can build the resilience and state of mind required to overcome setbacks, because Stoics teach turning obstacles into opportunity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A lesson every business entrepreneur needs to learn.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I would argue that studying Stoicism is as relevant today as it was 2,000 years ago, thanks to its brilliant insights into how to lead a good life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At the very root of the thinking, there is a very simple way of living.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Control what you can, and accept what you can't.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is not as easy as it sounds, and will require considerable practice.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It can take a lifetime to master.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Stoics also believed the most important foundation for a good and happy life is not money, fame, power or pleasure, but having a disciplined and principled character.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Something which seems to resonate with many people today.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Stoicism
